The noisy input "rolls downhill" into the nearest local minimum — which may be a stored pattern or an unwanted **spurious state**. Lower energy valleys are deeper attractors corresponding to the learned prototypes. ## 4.4 Algorithm Flowchart — Asynchronous State Update Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Initialize:
Set all neuron
states to query
pattern S0"] --> B["Pick a neuron k
randomly or
in cyclic order"] B --> C["Compute local field
hk = sum over j not k
of wkj sj + bk"] C --> D{"Sign of hk?"} D -- "hk >= 0" --> E["Set sk = +1"] D -- "hk < 0" --> F["Set sk = -1"] E --> G{"Any state
change in
this sweep?"} F --> G G -- "Yes" --> B G -- "No" --> H["CONVERGED:
Output stable state
S star as recalled memory"] classDef decision fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#A16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#713F12 classDef state fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A classDef terminal fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#064E3B class B,D,G decision class A,C,E,F state class H terminal ``` **Caption:** Algorithmic procedure for asynchronous recall. The loop terminates only when a full sweep over all neurons produces no state change — the formal definition of a fixed point. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a Discrete Hopfield Network. List any four salient features of its architecture.** (3 marks) **Model Answer:** A Discrete Hopfield Network is a single-layer, fully recurrent feedback neural network with binary/bipolar threshold neurons and symmetric, zero-diagonal weights, designed to function as a content-addressable associative memory. **Four salient features:** 1. **Fully recurrent topology:** Every neuron is connected to every other neuron with symmetric weights ($w_{ij} = w_{ji}$). 2. **No self-connections:** $w_{ii} = 0$ for all $i$, preventing self-reinforcement. 3. **Binary/bipolar states:** Each neuron state $s_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ (or $\{0,1\}$). 4. **Guaranteed convergence:** Possesses a Lyapunov energy function that monotonically decreases under asynchronous updates, ensuring convergence to a stable fixed point. > **Marking scheme:** Definition 1 mark + 4 features × 0.5 mark = 3 marks. --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Write the expression for the energy (Lyapunov) function of a Discrete Hopfield Network. State the role of this function in proving convergence.** (3 marks) **Model Answer:** **Energy function expression:** $$E = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{N} w_{ij} s_i s_j \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i s_i$$ **Role in convergence proof:** The energy function $E$ is a **Lyapunov function** for the network dynamics. Under asynchronous state updates $s_k \rightarrow \text{sgn}(h_k)$, the energy change is: $$\Delta E = -\Delta s_k \cdot h_k \leq 0$$ Since the state space is finite ($2^N$ states) and $E$ is bounded below, repeated updates must terminate at a state where no change occurs — a **stable fixed point**. This guarantees convergence from any initial state. > **Marking scheme:** Equation 1.5 marks + Role explanation 1.5 marks = 3 marks. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Discrete Hopfield Network. Discuss the significance of the symmetry condition $w_{ij} = w_{ji}$ and the zero self-connection $w_{ii} = 0$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the weight update rule for storing a single bipolar pattern $\mathbf{S}^{1} = (s_1, s_2, \dots, s_N)$ in a Discrete Hopfield Network. Show mathematically that the stored pattern is a stable state of the network. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** ### Part (a) — Architecture & Symmetry **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A(("s1")) -- w12 --> B(("s2")) B -- w21 --> A A -- w13 --> C(("s3")) C -- w31 --> A B -- w23 --> C C -- w32 --> B classDef n fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px class A,B,C n ``` **Explanation:** A Discrete Hopfield Network consists of $N$ binary/bipolar neurons arranged in a single layer with **complete interconnection**: each neuron $i$ is connected to every other neuron $j \neq i$ via a symmetric weight $w_{ij}$. There is no input layer, no output layer, and no hidden layer — the same neurons serve as both input (initial state) and output (recalled state). **Significance of $w_{ij} = w_{ji}$:** The symmetry of the weight matrix is **the fundamental condition** that allows the construction of the Lyapunov energy function: $$E = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i}\sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} s_i s_j - \sum_i b_i s_i$$ Without symmetry, the double-sum term in the energy derivation would not be well-defined, and the proof $\Delta E \leq 0$ would fail. Symmetry ensures that the energy change under an asynchronous update is: $$\Delta E = -\Delta s_k \sum_{j \neq k} w_{kj} s_j = -\Delta s_k \, h_k \leq 0$$ **Significance of $w_{ii} = 0$:** Self-connections are forbidden because they would create a positive self-energy term $w_{ii} s_i^2 = w_{ii}$ that is constant but adds noise to the local field $h_i$. Conceptually, a neuron should respond to *other* neurons, not to its own state, to prevent trivial self-stabilization and ensure genuine collective dynamics. (7 marks distribution: Diagram 2, Architecture 2, Symmetry 1.5, Zero self 1.5) --- ### Part (b) — Weight Update Derivation & Stability **Step 1: Single-pattern storage rule.** For a single bipolar prototype $\mathbf{S}^{1} = (s_1, s_2, \dots, s_N)$, the Hebbian outer product (with $N$-normalization ignored for simplicity) is: $$w_{ij} = s_i \cdot s_1 \cdot s_j \cdot s_1 = s_i s_j \quad \text{for } i \neq j, \qquad w_{ii} = 0$$ **Step 2: Verify $\mathbf{S}^{1}$ is a fixed point.** Compute the local field at neuron $i$ when the network is in state $\mathbf{S}^{1}$: $$h_i = \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{N} w_{ij} s_j = \sum_{j \neq i} (s_i s_j) s_j = s_i \sum_{j \neq i} s_j^2 = s_i (N-1)$$ **Step 3: Apply the activation function.** $$s_i^{\text{new}} = \text{sgn}(h_i) = \text{sgn}(s_i (N-1)) = \text{sgn}(s_i) = s_i \quad (\text{since } N \geq 2)$$ **Step 4: Stability check.** $$h_i \cdot s_i = s_i (N-1) \cdot s_i = (N-1) > 0 \quad \forall i$$ This satisfies the stability condition $h_i \cdot s_i > 0$, confirming that $\mathbf{S}^{1}$ is a **stable fixed point** of the network dynamics. The pattern will be recalled exactly when the network is initialized at $\mathbf{S}^{1}$. $\blacksquare$ (7 marks: Hebbian rule 2, Field derivation 2, Sign application 1.5, Stability conclusion 1.5) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** State and explain the energy function of a Discrete Hopfield Network. Prove that under asynchronous state updates, the energy is monotonically non-increasing. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a 4-neuron Discrete Hopfield Network, store the bipolar pattern $\mathbf{S}^{1} = (+1, -1, +1, -1)$ using the Hebbian rule. Then initialize the network with the noisy pattern $\mathbf{S}^{(0)} = (-1, -1, +1, -1)$ and perform one complete asynchronous update sweep. Show whether the noisy input converges to the stored pattern. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** ### Part (a) — Energy Function & Monotonicity Proof **Statement of energy function:** $$E(\mathbf{S}) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{N} w_{ij} s_i s_j \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i s_i$$ **Explanation:** This scalar function maps every state of the network to a real number. The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ prevents double-counting of the symmetric pairwise terms. The energy is bounded above (by the maximum weight sum) and below (by a finite minimum), so the dynamics cannot cycle indefinitely. **Proof of monotonicity under asynchronous updates:** Consider neuron $k$ flipping from $s_k^{\text{old}}$ to $s_k^{\text{new}} = -s_k^{\text{old}}$ (i.e., $\Delta s_k = -2 s_k^{\text{old}} = +2 s_k^{\text{new}}$). The change in energy involves only the terms containing $s_k$: $$\Delta E = E^{\text{new}} - E^{\text{old}} = -\sum_{j \neq k} w_{kj} s_j \, \Delta s_k - b_k \, \Delta s_k$$ $$\Delta E = -\Delta s_k \left( \sum_{j \neq k} w_{kj} s_j + b_k \right) = -\Delta s_k \cdot h_k$$ By the update rule, $s_k^{\text{new}} = \text{sgn}(h_k)$, which forces $\Delta s_k$ and $h_k$ to have the **same sign** (or $\Delta s_k = 0$ if no flip is needed). Therefore: $$\Delta s_k \cdot h_k \geq 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \Delta E \leq 0$$ with equality only when no state change occurs. This proves that $E$ is monotonically non-increasing. Since the state space is finite, the process must terminate at a local minimum — a stable fixed point. $\blacksquare$ (7 marks: Statement 1, Explanation 1.5, Derivation 3, Conclusion 1.5) --- ### Part (b) — Worked Numerical Example **Step 1: Compute weights using Hebbian rule for $\mathbf{S}^{1} = (+1, -1, +1, -1)$:** $$w_{12} = s_1 s_2 = (+1)(-1) = -1, \quad w_{13} = s_1 s_3 = (+1)(+1) = +1$$ $$w_{14} = s_1 s_4 = (+1)(-1) = -1, \quad w_{23} = s_2 s_3 = (-1)(+1) = -1$$ $$w_{24} = s_2 s_4 = (-1)(-1) = +1, \quad w_{34} = s_3 s_4 = (+1)(-1) = -1$$ By symmetry: $w_{ji} = w_{ij}$, and $w_{ii} = 0$ for all $i$. **Step 2: Initialize with noisy input $\mathbf{S}^{(0)} = (-1, -1, +1, -1)$:** **Update neuron 1** (cycle order: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4): $$h_1 = w_{12} s_2 + w_{13} s_3 + w_{14} s_4 = (-1)(-1) + (+1)(+1) + (-1)(-1) = 1 + 1 + 1 = +3$$ $$s_1^{\text{new}} = \text{sgn}(+3) = +1$$ **Update neuron 2:** $$h_2 = w_{21} s_1^{\text{new}} + w_{23} s_3 + w_{24} s_4 = (-1)(+1) + (-1)(+1) + (+1)(-1) = -1 - 1 - 1 = -3$$ $$s_2^{\text{new}} = \text{sgn}(-3) = -1$$ **Update neuron 3:** $$h_3 = w_{31} s_1^{\text{new}} + w_{32} s_2^{\text{new}} + w_{34} s_4 = (+1)(+1) + (-1)(-1) + (-1)(-1) = 1 + 1 + 1 = +3$$ $$s_3^{\text{new}} = \text{sgn}(+3) = +1$$ **Update neuron 4:** $$h_4 = w_{41} s_1^{\text{new}} + w_{42} s_2^{\text{new}} + w_{43} s_3^{\text{new}} = (-1)(+1) + (+1)(-1) + (-1)(+1) = -1 - 1 - 1 = -3$$ $$s_4^{\text{new}} = \text{sgn}(-3) = -1$$ **Final state after one sweep:** $\mathbf{S}^{(1)} = (+1, -1, +1, -1) = \mathbf{S}^{1}$ ✓ **Step 3: Convergence verification:** Since $\mathbf{S}^{(1)} = \mathbf{S}^{1}$, a second sweep produces no further changes, confirming that the stored pattern has been successfully recalled from the noisy input. (7 marks: Weights 2, Initialization 1, Sweep 3, Conclusion 1) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Discrete Hopfield Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting $w_{ii} = 0$:** Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for omitting the zero-diagonal constraint. > 2. **Using unipolar states instead of bipolar:** The Hebbian rule $w_{ij} = s_i s_j$ only works for $s_i \in \{-1,+1\}$. If states are $\{0,1\}$, students must use $w_{ij} = (2s_i - 1)(2s_j - 1)$ — this is a **favourite trick question** in KTU. > 3. **Confusing synchronous with asynchronous:** The energy proof $\Delta E \leq 0$ **only holds for asynchronous updates**. Claiming the same for synchronous updates loses marks. > 4. **Missing the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor in energy:** Students often write the energy without the $\tfrac{1}{2}$, double-counting pairwise terms. This leads to a factor-of-2 error in crosstalk calculations. > 5. **Capacity formula confusion:** Original Hopfield capacity is $0.138N$ (uncorrelated patterns), not $\tfrac{N}{2\ln N}$ (Amit et al. asymptotic). KTU prefers the original $0.138N$ formula. > 6. **Stability vs. recall confusion:** A pattern being a fixed point is **necessary but not sufficient** for large-radius recall. Examiners may ask about the *basin of attraction* — students should mention Hamming distance. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Discrete Hopfield Networks** - [x] **Definition:** Recurrent, single-layer, binary/bipolar network with symmetric weights and zero self-connections. - [x] **Purpose:** Content-addressable associative memory (CAM) — recalls full pattern from partial/noisy cue. - [x] **State update rule:** $s_i^{(t+1)} = \text{sgn}\!\left(\sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} s_j^{(t)} + b_i\right)$ (asynchronous) or synchronous version. - [x] **Hebbian weight rule:** $w_{ij} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{\mu=1}^{P} s_i^{\mu} s_j^{\mu}$, with $w_{ii} = 0$. - [x] **Energy function:** $E = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i \neq j} w_{ij} s_i s_j - \sum_i b_i s_i$. - [x] **Energy monotonicity:** $\Delta E = -\Delta s_k \cdot h_k \leq 0$ under asynchronous updates. - [x] **Convergence:** Guaranteed by finite state space + Lyapunov function. - [x] **Storage capacity:** $\approx 0.138\,N$ patterns (original Hopfield estimate). - [x] **Symmetry condition:** $w_{ij} = w_{ji}$ is essential for energy-based convergence proof. - [x] **Spurious states:** Local minima that are not stored patterns; appear when $P > 0.138N$ or with correlated patterns. - [x] **Synchronous vs. asynchronous:** Asynchronous = guaranteed convergence; synchronous = may oscillate. - [x] **Bias term $b_i$:** Shifts activation threshold; often set to zero for simplicity in textbook examples. - [x] **Engineering applications:** Pattern denoising, completion, optimization (TSP via Hopfield-Tank), constraint satisfaction, image restoration. - [x] **Connection to Module 2 theme:** Discrete Hopfield Net is the canonical **feedback network** — the recurrent counterpart to feedforward MLPs studied earlier in the module. - [x] **Key 1982 reference:** John J. Hopfield, PNAS — *Neural networks and physical systems with emergent collective computational abilities*. ---

topics.title: Feed Forward Networks: Back Propagation Networks - Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT604 - SOFT COMPUTING AND COMPUTER VISION FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feed Forward Networks: Back Propagation Networks - Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Feed Forward Network (FFN):** A class of Artificial Neural Network (ANN) in which information moves in only one direction — from the input nodes, through any hidden layers, to the output nodes — with **no cycles or feedback loops**. The architecture forms a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back Propagation Network (BPN):** A *supervised-learning*, *multi-layer*, *fully-connected* Feed Forward Network trained using the **Error Back Propagation Algorithm** (popularised by Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams, 1986). During *training*, the architecture permits a *reverse* flow of **error gradients** from output → input, while the *inference* (recall) phase remains strictly feed-forward.

Architecturally, a BPN is composed of:
1. An **Input Layer** — passive distributors of the input feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.
2. One or more **Hidden Layers** — active computational layers containing neurons with non-linear activation functions.
3. An **Output Layer** — produces the network's prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

The *universal approximation theorem* (Cybenko, 1989; Hornik, 1991) guarantees that an FFN with **at least one hidden layer** and a sufficiently large number of neurons using a **sigmoidal** (or any squashing) activation can approximate **any continuous function** on a compact domain to arbitrary precision.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Quality Control Committee"**
>
> Imagine a factory that decides whether to *accept* or *reject* a manufactured part:
>
> - **Input Layer (Raw Inspectors):** Each raw inspector only reports a single measurement (length, weight, surface roughness…). They don't decide anything; they just hand over their data.
> - **Hidden Layer (Junior Managers):** Each junior manager *combines* the inspectors' reports using their own personal "weights" of importance, sums them, applies a threshold (activation function), and forwards a single decision signal. They cannot see the raw part; they only see the inspectors' reports.
> - **Output Layer (Senior Manager):** The senior manager combines all junior managers' reports and gives the final verdict.
>
> If the final verdict is wrong (e.g., a defective part is approved), the company doesn't fire the whole team — it traces the blame **backwards**. Each person adjusts how much they trust each subordinate's report. That backward blame-tracing is the **Back Propagation** of error.

In the robot perception context (course PBBRT604), this same structure is used to map raw camera pixels or LIDAR scans to object classes — feed-forward during inference, back-propagated error during training.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Parameters (Must Be Memorised)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Notation |
|---|---|---|
| $L$ | Total number of layers (excluding input) | $L = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ |
| $n^{[l]}$ | Number of neurons in layer $l$ | $n^{[0]}=n$, $n^{[L]}=m$ |
| $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}$ | Weight matrix from layer $l-1$ to layer $l$ | Shape: $(n^{[l]} \times n^{[l-1]})$ |
| $\mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ | Bias vector of layer $l$ | Shape: $(n^{[l]} \times 1)$ |
| $\mathbf{a}^{[l]}$ | Activation (output) vector of layer $l$ | $\mathbf{a}^{[0]}=\mathbf{x}$ |
| $\mathbf{z}^{[l]}$ | Pre-activation (net input) of layer $l$ | $\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]}\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ |
| $g(\cdot)$ | Element-wise activation function | Sigmoid, tanh, ReLU |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this topic is often asked as a 7 or 14 mark descriptive/derivation question, with the primary cognitive level being **Understand (L2)** and **Apply (L3)** under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single Artificial Neuron (The Building Block)

Every neuron in a BPN performs the *same* two-step mathematical operation:

1. **Weighted Summation (Linear Stage):** Combine all incoming inputs, multiply each by a weight, sum them, and add a bias.
2. **Non-linear Activation (Non-linear Stage):** Squash the sum through a non-linear function $g(\cdot)$.

For a single neuron receiving $n$ inputs $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$:

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$

$$a = g(z)$$

### Vectorised Form (One Layer of $n^{[l]}$ Neurons)

For layer $l$ with $n^{[l]}$ neurons receiving the activation vector $\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]}$ from the previous layer:

$$\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]} \, \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$$

$$\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = g\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{[l]}\right)$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{W}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{\,n^{[l]} \times n^{[l-1]}}$ — **weight matrix** of layer $l$.
- $\mathbf{b}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{\,n^{[l]}}$ — **bias vector** of layer $l$.
- $\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} \in \mathbb{R}^{\,n^{[l-1]}}$ — **output of the previous layer** (acts as input to the current layer).
- $g(\cdot)$ is applied **element-wise**.

## 2.2 Information Flow — The Two Phases of a BPN

A BPN has a strict bi-phase information regime:

| Phase | Direction of Information | Trigger | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Pass (Recall / Inference)** | Input $\rightarrow$ Output | A new input $\mathbf{x}$ is presented | Compute prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ |
| **Backward Pass (Training)** | Output $\rightarrow$ Input | Predicted $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is compared to true $\mathbf{y}$ | Update weights using gradient descent to minimise loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Point:** During the backward pass, the *same* network topology (connections) is reused in *reverse*. The "architecture" itself is unchanging — only the weights are updated. The phrase "Back Propagation" refers to the *algorithm*, not a different network shape.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol-by-Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]}\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ | Pre-activation (net input) of layer $l$ | Foundation of forward pass |
| 2 | $\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = g(\mathbf{z}^{[l]})$ | Output (activation) of layer $l$ | Introduces non-linearity |
| 3 | $\mathbf{a}^{[0]} = \mathbf{x}$ | Input layer activation = input vector | Boundary condition |
| 4 | $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{[L]}$ | Final output of $L$-layer network | Network's prediction |
| 5 | $J(\mathbf{W},\mathbf{b}) = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\mathcal{L}\!\left(\hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(i)}, \mathbf{y}^{(i)}\right)$ | Cost function over $m$ training samples | Objective to minimise during training |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}} = \delta^{[l]} \left(\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]}\right)^{\!\top}$ | Gradient of cost w.r.t. weights of layer $l$ | Drives weight update |
| 7 | $\delta^{[L]} = \left(\mathbf{a}^{[L]} - \mathbf{y}\right) \odot g'(\mathbf{z}^{[L]})$ | Error term at output layer | Back-prop starting point |
| 8 | $\delta^{[l]} = \left(\left(\mathbf{W}^{[l+1]}\right)^{\!\top}\delta^{[l+1]}\right) \odot g'(\mathbf{z}^{[l]})$ | Error term at hidden layer $l$ | Recursive backward propagation |
| 9 | $\mathbf{W}^{[l]} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{[l]} - \eta\,\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}}$ | Gradient descent weight update | Training rule |
| 10 | $\mathbf{b}^{[l]} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}^{[l]} - \eta\,\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{[l]}}$ | Bias update rule | Training rule |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam tables, always use the symbol $\eta$ for the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter, typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$). The symbol $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**.

## 2.4 Standard Activation Functions Used in BPN

| Function | Equation $g(z)$ | Derivative $g'(z)$ | Range | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid (Logistic) | $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $g(z)\!\left(1-g(z)\right)$ | $(0, 1)$ | Binary output, shallow nets |
| Hyperbolic Tangent | $\tanh(z)$ | $1-\tanh^{2}(z)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Hidden layers (zero-centred) |
| ReLU | $\max(0, z)$ | $\begin{cases}1, & z>0\\0, & z\le 0\end{cases}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Modern deep nets, avoids vanishing gradient |
| Softmax | $\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | — (Jacobian) | $(0,1)$, $\sum = 1$ | Multi-class output layer |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vanishing Gradient Pitfall:** Pure sigmoid/tanh activations in *deep* BPNs cause gradients to shrink exponentially as they flow backward. For KTU derivations, if the question says "3 or more hidden layers", the **expected answer key usually recommends ReLU for hidden layers and Sigmoid/Softmax for output only**.

## 2.5 Why "Fully Connected" Defines BPN Architecture

In a classical BPN, **every neuron in layer $l$ is connected to every neuron in layer $l-1$**. This gives:

$$\text{Number of weight parameters} = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \left(n^{[l]} \times n^{[l-1]} + n^{[l]}\right)$$

The first term is the weight count, the second is the bias count. For example, a 3-layer BPN with architecture $n^{[0]}=4, n^{[1]}=5, n^{[2]}=3, n^{[3]}=2$ has:

$$(4\times5 + 5) + (5\times3 + 3) + (3\times2 + 2) = 25 + 18 + 8 = 51 \text{ parameters}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This dense connectivity is what gives BPNs their power to model *any* non-linear function, but it is also their *cost* — large parameter counts demand more training data, more memory, and risk of overfitting. This is why Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) replace dense layers for image input in Module 3 / 4 of this course.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of BPN Architecture

- **Robotics (this course's context):** BPNs are used in end-effector position control, inverse kinematics, and sensor-fusion preprocessing for SLAM.
- **Computer Vision:** Pre-deep-learning era — BPNs were used for hand-written digit recognition (MNIST), face recognition, optical character recognition.
- **Medical Diagnosis:** Early-warning systems (e.g., breast cancer, diabetes prediction) from tabular clinical data.
- **Control Systems:** Model-reference adaptive control where a BPN learns the inverse dynamics of a plant.
- **Financial Forecasting:** Time-series prediction where the architecture of Section 3 is replicated across time steps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Pass of a Single Neuron (from First Principles)

**Starting premise:** A single artificial neuron receives $n$ input signals, each scaled by an associated weight, with an additive bias, followed by an activation.

### Step 1 — Define the input vector and weight vector

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} w_1 \\ w_2 \\ \vdots \\ w_n \end{bmatrix}$$

**Logic:** Every input signal $x_i$ has a corresponding importance factor $w_i$. Bias $b$ is treated as a *constant* offset.

### Step 2 — Compute the weighted sum (dot product)

$$\begin{aligned}
z &= w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_n x_n + b \\
  &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b \\
  &= \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** The dot product $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ is a scalar that quantifies the *total stimulation* the neuron receives. The bias $b$ shifts the activation threshold.

### Step 3 — Apply the non-linear activation

$$a = g(z) = g\!\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b\right)$$

**Logic:** Without $g(\cdot)$, stacking multiple layers would collapse into a single linear transformation, regardless of depth. The non-linearity is the *mathematical source* of the network's representational power.

### Step 4 — Declare this as the neuron's output

The scalar $a$ is the *output of the neuron* and simultaneously the *input to the next layer's neurons*.

> **Conclusion (single neuron):**
> $$\boxed{\,a = g\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forward Pass of an Entire BPN (Layer-by-Layer)

**Starting premise:** Consider an $L$-layer BPN with input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and output vector $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$.

### Step 1 — Define layer-wise parameters

For every layer $l = 1, 2, \dots, L$:
- $\mathbf{W}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{\,n^{[l]}\times n^{[l-1]}}$ — weight matrix.
- $\mathbf{b}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{\,n^{[l]}}$ — bias vector.
- $g^{[l]}(\cdot)$ — activation function of layer $l$.

### Step 2 — Boundary condition: layer 0 (the input "layer")

$$\mathbf{a}^{[0]} = \mathbf{x}$$

**Logic:** The input layer does not compute — it merely holds the input. By convention, its "activation" is the input vector itself.

### Step 3 — Recursive forward propagation for layer 1

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{z}^{[1]} &= \mathbf{W}^{[1]}\,\mathbf{a}^{[0]} + \mathbf{b}^{[1]} = \mathbf{W}^{[1]}\,\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{[1]} \\
\mathbf{a}^{[1]} &= g^{[1]}\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{[1]}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Substitute $\mathbf{a}^{[0]}$ with $\mathbf{x}$. The matrix-vector product $\mathbf{W}^{[1]}\mathbf{x}$ produces an $n^{[1]}$-vector of net inputs, one per hidden neuron.

### Step 4 — Generic recursion for layer $l$

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{z}^{[l]} &= \mathbf{W}^{[l]}\,\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]} \\
\mathbf{a}^{[l]} &= g^{[l]}\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{[l]}\right), \qquad l = 1, 2, \dots, L
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** This is the master equation of the forward pass. Each layer's output becomes the next layer's input. The recursion terminates at $l = L$.

### Step 5 — Final output

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{[L]}$$

**Logic:** The final layer's activation is the network's prediction. The choice of $g^{[L]}$ depends on the task:
- **Sigmoid** for binary classification.
- **Softmax** for multi-class classification.
- **Linear (identity)** for regression.

### Step 6 — Composite expression (unrolled)

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{y}} = g^{[L]}\!\Big( &\mathbf{W}^{[L]} \, g^{[L-1]}\!\big(\mathbf{W}^{[L-1]} \, \cdots \, g^{[1]}(\mathbf{W}^{[1]}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{[1]}) \cdots + \mathbf{b}^{[L-1]}\big) + \mathbf{b}^{[L]}\Big)
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion (full forward pass):**
> $$\boxed{\,\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{[L]} = g^{[L]}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{[L]}\mathbf{a}^{[L-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[L]}\right) = g^{[L]}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{[L]}\,g^{[L-1]}\!\left(\cdots\,g^{[1]}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{[1]}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{[1]}\right)\cdots\right) + \mathbf{b}^{[L]}\right)\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Counting the Parameters of a BPN

**Starting premise:** A BPN with architecture $n^{[0]} \to n^{[1]} \to n^{[2]} \to \cdots \to n^{[L]}$.

### Step 1 — Count weights in layer $l$

A weight matrix $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}$ of shape $(n^{[l]} \times n^{[l-1]})$ contains exactly $n^{[l]} \times n^{[l-1]}$ entries.

### Step 2 — Count biases in layer $l$

A bias vector $\mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ of length $n^{[l]}$ contains exactly $n^{[l]}$ entries.

### Step 3 — Sum over all $L$ layers

$$\text{Total Parameters} = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \left(n^{[l]} \times n^{[l-1]} + n^{[l]}\right) = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \left(n^{[l]}\!\left(n^{[l-1]} + 1\right)\right)$$

### Step 4 — Worked example

For the architecture $4 \to 5 \to 3 \to 2$ (i.e., $n^{[0]}=4, n^{[1]}=5, n^{[2]}=3, n^{[3]}=2, L=3$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Parameters} &= \left(5 \times 4 + 5\right) + \left(3 \times 5 + 3\right) + \left(2 \times 3 + 2\right) \\
&= (20 + 5) + (15 + 3) + (6 + 2) \\
&= 25 + 18 + 8 \\
&= 51
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion:** This BPN has **51 learnable parameters**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example of a Forward Pass

**Architecture:** $n^{[0]}=2 \to n^{[1]}=2 \to n^{[2]}=1$ with sigmoid activation everywhere.

$$\mathbf{W}^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b}^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{W}^{[2]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b}^{[2]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Input:** $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$

### Step 1 — Compute layer 1 pre-activation

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{z}^{[1]} &= \mathbf{W}^{[1]}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{[1]} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply sigmoid activation

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{a}^{[1]} &= \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{[1]}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-0.6}} \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-0.6}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6457 \\ 0.6457 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute layer 2 pre-activation

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{z}^{[2]} &= \mathbf{W}^{[2]}\mathbf{a}^{[1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[2]} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.6457 \\ 0.6457 \end{bmatrix} + 0.2 \\
&= (0.6 \times 0.6457) + (0.9 \times 0.6457) + 0.2 \\
&= 0.3874 + 0.5811 + 0.2 = 1.1685
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Final sigmoid output

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(1.1685) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.1685}} \approx 0.7630$$

> **Conclusion:** The network outputs $\hat{y} \approx 0.763$, which would, in a binary classification context, round to class **1**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level BPN Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The diagram below maps the **information flow** of a generic 3-layer Back Propagation Network (one hidden layer). It is *not* a physical schematic but a **functional block diagram** showing how signals propagate, how error flows back, and how weights are updated.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INPUT LAYER n0 neurons Passive distributors Receives feature vector x"] --> B["HIDDEN LAYER 1 n1 neurons Computes z1 = W1 a0 + b1 Outputs a1 = g z1"]
    B --> C["OUTPUT LAYER n2 neurons Computes z2 = W2 a1 + b2 Outputs yhat = a2 = g z2"]
    C --> D["LOSS FUNCTION Compares yhat with true y Returns scalar J"]
    D --> E["OUTPUT ERROR delta2 = yhat - y times g prime z2"]
    E --> F["HIDDEN ERROR delta1 = W2 transposed times delta2 elementwise g prime z1"]
    F --> G["GRADIENT DESCENT UPDATE W1 b1 W2 b2 move opposite to gradient with step eta"]
    G --> B
    G --> C
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram in a KTU exam:** During a viva or 7-mark descriptive answer, narrate the flow *left → right* for forward pass and *right → left* along the same connections (dotted/back edges in your sketch) for back-propagation. The architecture itself never changes; only the weights $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}$ and biases $\mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ get updated.

## 4.2 Detailed Neuron-Level Schematic of a 2-2-1 BPN

The following diagram zooms into a small $2 \to 2 \to 1$ BPN showing **every connection** with its associated weight.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1(("x1")) -->|w11_1| H1A["H1_1 z = w11_1 x1 + w12_1 x2 + b1_1 a = g z"]
    X2(("x2")) -->|w12_1| H1A
    X1 -->|w21_1| H1B["H1_2 z = w21_1 x1 + w22_1 x2 + b2_1 a = g z"]
    X2 -->|w22_1| H1B
    H1A -->|w11_2| O1["Out z = w11_2 a1 + w12_2 a2 + b1_2 yhat = g z"]
    H1B -->|w12_2| O1
```

> **Annotation for the exam:** In this diagram, $w_{ij}^{[l]}$ denotes the weight from neuron $j$ in layer $l-1$ to neuron $i$ in layer $l$. There are $(2 \times 2) + (2 \times 1) = 6$ weights and $(2 + 1) = 3$ biases, for a total of **9 parameters**.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow of the BPN Training Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["STEP 1 INITIALISE W and b Random or Xavier He"] --> S2["STEP 2 FORWARD PASS Compute yhat layer by layer"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 LOSS Compute J W b between yhat and y"]
    S3 --> S4{"STEP 4 CHECK Converged J less than epsilon"}
    S4 -->|No| S5["STEP 5 BACKWARD PASS Compute delta for each layer L to 1"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6 UPDATE W l and b l using gradient descent with learning rate eta"]
    S6 --> S2
    S4 -->|Yes| S7["STEP 7 STOP Network is trained Ready for inference"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked to "explain the BPN architecture", always present the diagram **first**, then narrate the forward pass, then the backward pass, then list the parameters and activation functions. Marks are typically awarded 2 for diagram, 3 for forward pass, 3 for backward/learning rule, 3 for activation/parameter discussion, 3 for neatness/terminology in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) regulations, Part A questions are *direct, low-cognitive-level* (Remember/Understand) and must be answered in **under 6 minutes** with crisp, terminologically precise answers.

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a Back Propagation Network (BPN). List its three architectural layers with their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Back Propagation Network (BPN)** is a multi-layer, fully connected, feed-forward Artificial Neural Network trained using the supervised *Error Back Propagation Algorithm* to minimise a differentiable loss function.

It consists of three architectural layers:

| Layer | Function |
|---|---|
| **Input Layer** | Receives the feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. Passive — performs no computation, only distributes the values. |
| **Hidden Layer(s)** | Performs weighted summation $\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]}\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ followed by non-linear activation $\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = g(\mathbf{z}^{[l]})$. Extracts intermediate feature representations. |
| **Output Layer** | Produces the final prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{[L]}$; the choice of $g^{[L]}$ depends on the task (sigmoid, softmax, linear). |

During training, the *error* at the output is propagated *backwards* through the same connections to update the weights via gradient descent.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**Differentiate between Feed Forward Network and Recurrent Neural Network in terms of architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Feed Forward Network (FFN/BPN) | Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Information flow** | Strictly one-way: input → output (Directed Acyclic Graph) | Bidirectional in time; allows cycles/loops (Directed Cyclic Graph) |
| **Memory** | No memory of past inputs; each input is processed independently | Maintains hidden state → retains memory of past inputs |
| **Use case** | Static pattern classification, regression | Sequence data (time series, speech, NLP) |
| **Training** | Standard back-propagation | Back-Propagation Through Time (BPTT) |
| **Example architectures** | MLP, BPN, deep feed-forward nets | Elman, Jordan, LSTM, GRU |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE Module 2 pattern, Part B questions carry **14 marks** split into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt **one full question** (either Q-A or Q-B). The two choices are *fully independent alternatives* drawn from the same module weightage cluster.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **general architecture of a Back Propagation Network**. Explain the role of each layer and the significance of the *fully connected* property. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **mathematical model of a single artificial neuron** showing both the linear (weighted summation) and non-linear (activation) stages. Explain why the **non-linear activation function** is essential for the network's representational power. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A

**Part (a) — Architecture of a BPN [7 marks]:**

A BPN is a multi-layer, fully-connected feed-forward neural network trained with the error back-propagation algorithm. Its general architecture consists of three types of layers:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IL["INPUT LAYER n0 neurons x1 x2 xn"] --> HL["HIDDEN LAYER n1 neurons H1 H2 Hm Apply weights biases and activation g"]
    HL --> OL["OUTPUT LAYER n2 neurons y1 y2 yk"]
```

- **Input Layer [1 mark]:** Comprises $n^{[0]}$ neurons (one per feature). It does not perform any computation; it merely receives the input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^{\top}$ and distributes it to every neuron in the next layer.
- **Hidden Layer(s) [2 marks]:** Each neuron computes a weighted sum of all incoming activations, adds a bias, and applies a non-linear activation. With $n^{[1]}$ hidden neurons, the layer outputs a transformed vector $\mathbf{a}^{[1]} \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{[1]}}$.
- **Output Layer [1 mark]:** Produces the final prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{[L]} \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{[L]}}$.
- **Fully Connected Property [2 marks]:** In a BPN, every neuron in layer $l$ is connected to *every* neuron in layer $l-1$ via a unique weight. The total number of learnable parameters is
$$P = \sum_{l=1}^{L}\!\left(n^{[l]}\!\left(n^{[l-1]}+1\right)\right)$$
This dense connectivity gives the BPN enough parameters to model arbitrary non-linear mappings, as guaranteed by the **Universal Approximation Theorem** (Cybenko, 1989).
- **Neat labelled diagram [1 mark]:** A clearly drawn diagram with input/hidden/output layers and labelled weights (as in Section 4.2) is essential.

**Part (b) — Mathematical model of a single neuron [7 marks]:**

Consider a single neuron receiving $n$ inputs $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ with associated weights $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$ and bias $b$.

**Step 1 — Linear Aggregation (Weighted Sum) [2 marks]:**
$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b$$
**Logic:** Each input is scaled by a weight representing its importance; the bias shifts the activation threshold.

**Step 2 — Non-linear Activation [2 marks]:**
$$a = g(z) = g\!\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b\right)$$
**Logic:** A non-linear function $g$ (e.g., sigmoid, tanh, ReLU) squashes the linear output.

**Step 3 — Vectorised Multi-Neuron Form [1 mark]:** For a layer of $n^{[l]}$ neurons:
$$\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]}\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}, \qquad \mathbf{a}^{[l]} = g(\mathbf{z}^{[l]})$$

**Step 4 — Why non-linearity is essential [2 marks]:**
If $g(\cdot)$ were the identity (linear), then stacking $L$ layers would yield
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{W}^{[L]}\mathbf{W}^{[L-1]}\cdots\mathbf{W}^{[1]}\mathbf{x} + (\text{combined bias}) = \mathbf{W}_{\text{eff}}\,\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_{\text{eff}}$$
which is mathematically equivalent to a *single-layer* linear model — defeating the purpose of depth. Non-linearity is what allows deep networks to approximate complex, curved decision boundaries and arbitrary continuous functions (per the Universal Approximation Theorem).

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain, with equations, the **forward propagation phase** of a Back Propagation Network for an arbitrary $L$-layer architecture. Clearly define the weight matrix $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}$, the bias vector $\mathbf{b}^{[l]}$, the pre-activation $\mathbf{z}^{[l]}$, and the activation $\mathbf{a}^{[l]}$ for each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Tabulate any **four standard activation functions** used in BPNs, giving their equations, ranges, and a one-line use-case recommendation. Justify the choice of **sigmoid for the output** layer in a binary classification problem. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B

**Part (a) — Forward Propagation [7 marks]:**

Consider an $L$-layer BPN with input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and output $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$. Define the following for every layer $l = 1, 2, \dots, L$:

| Symbol | Shape | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}$ | $n^{[l]} \times n^{[l-1]}$ | Weight matrix connecting layer $l-1$ to layer $l$ |
| $\mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ | $n^{[l]} \times 1$ | Bias vector of layer $l$ |
| $\mathbf{z}^{[l]}$ | $n^{[l]} \times 1$ | Pre-activation (net input) of layer $l$ |
| $\mathbf{a}^{[l]}$ | $n^{[l]} \times 1$ | Post-activation (output) of layer $l$ |
| $g^{[l]}(\cdot)$ | scalar | Non-linear activation function of layer $l$ |

**Step 1 — Boundary condition [1 mark]:**
$$\mathbf{a}^{[0]} = \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2 — Recursive forward equations [3 marks]:**
For each layer $l = 1, 2, \dots, L$:
$$\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]}\,\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$$
$$\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = g^{[l]}\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{[l]}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Final output [1 mark]:**
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{[L]}$$

**Step 4 — Unrolled composite [2 marks]:**
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = g^{[L]}\!\Big(\mathbf{W}^{[L]}\,g^{[L-1]}\!\big(\mathbf{W}^{[L-1]}\cdots\,g^{[1]}(\mathbf{W}^{[1]}\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}^{[1]})+\cdots+\mathbf{b}^{[L-1]}\big)+\mathbf{b}^{[L]}\Big)$$
This composition of affine transformations and non-linearities is what gives the BPN its universal function-approximation capability.

**Part (b) — Activation Functions [7 marks]:**

| # | Function | Equation $g(z)$ | Range | Recommended Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | Output layer of binary classification |
| 2 | **Hyperbolic Tangent (tanh)** | $\dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Hidden layers (zero-centred → faster convergence) |
| 3 | **ReLU** | $\max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Hidden layers of deep networks (mitigates vanishing gradient) |
| 4 | **Softmax** | $\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K}e^{z_j}}$ | $(0,1)$, $\sum=1$ | Output layer of multi-class classification |

**Justification for sigmoid at output (binary classification) [3 marks]:**

The sigmoid function squashes any real-valued input to a probability-like scalar in $(0, 1)$. For binary classification, this can be directly interpreted as $\hat{P}(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})$, and the class label is decided by the threshold $\hat{y} \ge 0.5$. Additionally, sigmoid has a simple, well-behaved derivative $g'(z) = g(z)(1-g(z))$, which keeps the gradient computations in the back-propagation step clean and numerically stable — a key reason it has been the historical default for binary output neurons in BPNs.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in BPN-architecture questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Architecture" with "Algorithm":** The architecture is *static* (layers, neuron counts, connectivity). The back-propagation *algorithm* is dynamic. Examiners specifically deduct marks if a student describes only the training algorithm under the guise of "architecture".
> 2. **Missing the input-layer boundary condition:** Many students forget to state $\mathbf{a}^{[0]} = \mathbf{x}$, which is essential to anchor the recursion. This costs 1 mark.
> 3. **No diagram = automatic 2-mark penalty** in any 7- or 14-mark architecture question. Always draw a labelled block diagram.
> 4. **Bias omission:** The bias vector $\mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ is *not* optional. Marks are awarded for mentioning that each neuron has its own bias and that the bias vector is added to the weighted sum.
> 5. **Wrong matrix shapes:** Stating that $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}$ has shape $(n^{[l-1]} \times n^{[l]})$ instead of $(n^{[l]} \times n^{[l-1]})$ is a classic 1-mark deduction. Memorise: **rows = current layer, columns = previous layer**.
> 6. **Forgetting the role of $g(\cdot)$:** Many students only write $a = \sum w_i x_i + b$ and stop, earning partial credit. Always include the activation step and *justify* why it is non-linear.
> 7. **Activation function mismatch:** Using sigmoid in deep hidden layers without acknowledging the vanishing-gradient risk. Examiners in 2024 scheme prefer ReLU mentions for "deep" BPNs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Final 60-second pre-exam scan. Tick these mentally before submitting your answer sheet.

- [ ] A BPN is a **multi-layer, fully-connected, feed-forward** neural network trained with the **back-propagation** algorithm.
- [ ] Three layer types: **Input (passive), Hidden (active, non-linear), Output (task-specific activation).**
- [ ] **Master forward-pass equation:** $\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = g(\mathbf{W}^{[l]}\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]})$ for $l = 1, \dots, L$, with $\mathbf{a}^{[0]} = \mathbf{x}$.
- [ ] **Weight matrix shape:** $\mathbf{W}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{\,n^{[l]} \times n^{[l-1]}}$.
- [ ] **Bias vector shape:** $\mathbf{b}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{\,n^{[l]}}$.
- [ ] **Parameter count:** $\displaystyle P = \sum_{l=1}^{L} n^{[l]}(n^{[l-1]}+1)$.
- [ ] **Activation choices:** Sigmoid/Softmax for output, ReLU/tanh for hidden layers.
- [ ] **Cybenko's Universal Approximation Theorem:** a BPN with one hidden layer and enough neurons can approximate *any* continuous function on a compact domain.
- [ ] The *architecture* is fixed; only **weights and biases** are learned during training.
- [ ] **Forward pass = inference / recall**; **Backward pass = training (error gradient flow).**
- [ ] **Fully connected** means every neuron in layer $l$ connects to every neuron in layer $l-1$ — no skipping, no sharing.
- [ ] **Why non-linearity matters:** Without $g(\cdot)$, any stack of layers collapses to a single linear transformation.
- [ ] For the KTU exam, **always draw the diagram first** before writing any equations.
- [ ] In error back-propagation, output error $\delta^{[L]}$ is computed first, then $\delta^{[l-1]}$ is computed *recursively* using $\left(\mathbf{W}^{[l]}\right)^{\top}$.
- [ ] The learning rate $\eta$ controls the step size in gradient descent; too large → divergence, too small → slow convergence.
- [ ] For robotics (course context): BPNs are used in **inverse-kinematics learning, end-effector control, and sensor fusion preprocessing**.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Back Propagation Networks: Architecture**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern. Module 2 mapped to Course Outcomes CO2 and CO3 (Soft Computing fundamentals for robotics).*

topics.title: Training Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT604 - SOFT COMPUTING AND COMPUTER VISION FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Training Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Training Algorithm (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *training algorithm* in soft computing refers to the systematic, iterative mathematical procedure used to adjust the **synaptic weights**, **biases**, and **learning parameters** of an artificial neural network (ANN) so that the network's output progressively minimizes a predefined **error (loss/cost) function** between the predicted output and the desired (target) output. For multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs), the standard training algorithm is the **Backpropagation (BP) Algorithm** operating on the principle of **Generalized Delta Rule (GDR)** using **Gradient Descent optimization**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme for **PBBRT604 (Soft Computing and Computer Vision for Robots)**, Module 2 specifically emphasizes the **Backpropagation Training Algorithm (BPA)**, the **Generalized Delta Rule**, **learning paradigms (supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement)**, and **performance metrics** that quantify how "well-trained" a network is.

### Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Epoch** | One complete pass of the entire training dataset through the network |
| **Iteration** | One weight update step (one mini-batch processed) |
| **Learning Rate (η)** | Step size controlling magnitude of weight adjustment |
| **Momentum (α)** | Fraction of previous weight change added to current update |
| **Error Signal (δ)** | Local gradient at a neuron used to update its incoming weights |
| **Loss Function (E)** | Scalar metric (typically MSE or Cross-Entropy) quantifying prediction error |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Archer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Blind Archer Training to Hit the Bullseye**
>
> Imagine an archer (the neural network) who is blindfolded and must shoot arrows at a target (the desired output). Each shot is a **forward pass** — the archer fires based on current muscle memory (weights).
>
> - **The miss distance** is the **error (E)**.
> - A coach standing behind the archer whispers: *"You missed 3 cm to the right and 5 cm high"* — this is the **error signal (δ)** calculated via backpropagation.
> - The archer then nudges the bow grip and adjusts the arm angle by a small amount — this is the **weight update (Δw)**.
> - The size of the nudge is governed by the **learning rate (η)**. Too large and the archer overshoots; too small and learning is painfully slow.
> - After thousands of shots (**epochs**), the archer's arrows cluster tightly around the bullseye — the network has **converged**.
>
> This iterative "shoot → measure error → correct → repeat" loop is the **essence of the training algorithm**.

## 1.3 Why Training Algorithms Matter in Robotics (KTU Context)

In the context of **PBBRT604**, the training algorithm is the *learning engine* of every intelligent robotic system. Whether a robot arm learns inverse kinematics, a mobile robot learns obstacle avoidance, or a vision system learns object recognition — all rely on a training algorithm that:

1. **Minimizes prediction error** for unseen data (generalization).
2. **Extracts feature hierarchies** automatically from raw sensor inputs.
3. **Provides differentiable end-to-end learning** for control policies.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever asked to *define* the training algorithm, always mention **three keywords**: (1) **iterative**, (2) **error-minimization**, (3) **weight-adjustment via gradient descent**. Examiners reward these keywords.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture Prerequisite: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)

The backpropagation training algorithm operates on a **feed-forward MLP** with at least one hidden layer. The general structure is:

$$\text{Input Layer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Hidden Layer(s)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Output Layer}$$

- **Input Layer:** Distributes input vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ to the hidden layer.
- **Hidden Layer:** Computes weighted sum + bias, then applies an **activation function** $f(\cdot)$.
- **Output Layer:** Produces final output $\mathbf{y}$ (or $\mathbf{o}$) for comparison with target $\mathbf{t}$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Backpropagation Training Algorithm

The algorithm proceeds in **two phases** repeated for every training sample:

### **Phase A — Forward Pass (Signal Propagation)**

1. Present input vector $\mathbf{x}^{(p)}$ for pattern $p$ to the input layer.
2. Compute net input at each hidden neuron $j$:
$$net_j^{(p)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ji}^{(p)} x_i^{(p)} + b_j$$
3. Apply activation function (typically **sigmoid** or **tanh**) to produce hidden output:
$$h_j^{(p)} = f\left(net_j^{(p)}\right) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-net_j^{(p)}}}$$
4. Propagate $h_j^{(p)}$ to output layer; compute net input at output neuron $k$:
$$net_k^{(p)} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_{kj}^{(p)} h_j^{(p)} + b_k$$
5. Compute final output:
$$o_k^{(p)} = f\left(net_k^{(p)}\right)$$
6. Calculate the **error** for pattern $p$:
$$E^{(p)} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{L} \left(t_k^{(p)} - o_k^{(p)}\right)^2$$
7. Compute **total error** across all $P$ patterns:
$$E_{total} = \sum_{p=1}^{P} E^{(p)}$$

### **Phase B — Backward Pass (Error Backpropagation)**

1. Compute the **error signal (delta)** at each output neuron $k$:
$$\delta_k^{(p)} = \left(t_k^{(p)} - o_k^{(p)}\right) \cdot f'\left(net_k^{(p)}\right)$$
2. Propagate the error signal backwards to compute delta at each hidden neuron $j$:
$$\delta_j^{(p)} = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{L} w_{kj}^{(p)} \delta_k^{(p)}\right) \cdot f'\left(net_j^{(p)}\right)$$
3. Update weights between hidden and output layer:
$$\Delta w_{kj}^{(p)} = \eta \cdot \delta_k^{(p)} \cdot h_j^{(p)}$$
$$w_{kj}^{(new)} = w_{kj}^{(old)} + \Delta w_{kj}^{(p)}$$
4. Update weights between input and hidden layer:
$$\Delta w_{ji}^{(p)} = \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(p)} \cdot x_i^{(p)}$$
$$w_{ji}^{(new)} = w_{ji}^{(old)} + \Delta w_{ji}^{(p)}$$
5. Update biases similarly:
$$b_k^{(new)} = b_k^{(old)} + \eta \cdot \delta_k^{(p)}$$
$$b_j^{(new)} = b_j^{(old)} + \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(p)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Backpropagation"?** The error $\delta$ is computed at the *output* layer first, then *propagated backwards* layer-by-layer. The chain rule of calculus makes this mathematically rigorous. This is the **Generalized Delta Rule (GDR)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Common Symbol |
|---|----------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $net_j = \sum_i w_{ji} x_i + b_j$ | Net input at neuron $j$ | $net_j$ |
| 2 | $h_j = f(net_j)$ | Hidden neuron output (activation) | $h_j$ |
| 3 | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | **Sigmoid activation** | $f(\cdot)$ |
| 4 | $f'(x) = f(x)(1-f(x))$ | Derivative of sigmoid | $f'$ |
| 5 | $E^{(p)} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_k (t_k - o_k)^2$ | **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** per pattern | $E$ |
| 6 | $\delta_k = (t_k - o_k)\, f'(net_k)$ | Output-layer error signal | $\delta_k$ |
| 7 | $\delta_j = \left(\sum_k w_{kj}\delta_k\right) f'(net_j)$ | Hidden-layer error signal | $\delta_j$ |
| 8 | $\Delta w_{kj} = \eta \, \delta_k \, h_j$ | Weight update (hidden→output) | $\Delta w$ |
| 9 | $\Delta w_{ji} = \eta \, \delta_j \, x_i$ | Weight update (input→hidden) | $\Delta w$ |
| 10 | $w_{new} = w_{old} + \Delta w$ | Weight assignment rule | $w$ |
| 11 | $w_{new} = w_{old} + \eta \, \delta_k \, h_j + \alpha \, \Delta w_{old}$ | **Generalized Delta Rule with Momentum** | $w$ |
| 12 | $E_{total} = \sum_p E^{(p)}$ | Total training error | $E$ |
| 13 | $\eta \in (0, 1)$ | Typical learning rate range | $\eta$ |
| 14 | $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ | Typical momentum range | $\alpha$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule Reminder:** In all KTU answer scripts and LaTeX tables, **never** use $\vert x \vert$ with single pipes inside a markdown table row — always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to prevent table-breaking.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Training Algorithm

| Robotics Application | Role of Training Algorithm |
|----------------------|----------------------------|
| **Mobile robot navigation** | Learns obstacle-avoidance mapping from sonar/IR sensor data |
| **Robotic arm control** | Learns inverse kinematics $q = f^{-1}(x,y,z)$ from demonstrations |
| **Computer vision (Module context)** | CNNs trained via backpropagation for object/face recognition |
| **SLAM & path planning** | Neural approximators learn value functions for reinforcement learning |
| **Speech & gesture recognition** | Trains recurrent/LSTM networks to map temporal signals to actions |

The training algorithm is therefore the **core learning mechanism** of *every* soft-computing module covered in PBBRT604 — fuzzy inference, neural networks, genetic algorithms, and deep vision systems all rely on iterative weight/tuning update procedures that follow the same general philosophy of **error-driven parameter adjustment**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Why $\delta_k = (t_k - o_k) f'(net_k)$?

We begin from the per-pattern error:
$$E^{(p)} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{L} \left(t_k^{(p)} - o_k^{(p)}\right)^2$$

We need to find how $E$ changes with respect to a weight $w_{kj}$ between hidden neuron $j$ and output neuron $k$. Using the **chain rule of multivariable calculus**:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{kj}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial o_k} \cdot \frac{\partial o_k}{\partial net_k} \cdot \frac{\partial net_k}{\partial w_{kj}}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial o_k}$.

$$E = \frac{1}{2}(t_k - o_k)^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\partial E}{\partial o_k} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot (t_k - o_k) \cdot (-1) = -(t_k - o_k)$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial o_k}{\partial net_k}$.

Since $o_k = f(net_k)$, by definition of the derivative:
$$\frac{\partial o_k}{\partial net_k} = f'(net_k)$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial net_k}{\partial w_{kj}}$.

Since $net_k = \sum_j w_{kj} h_j + b_k$:
$$\frac{\partial net_k}{\partial w_{kj}} = h_j$$

**Step 4:** Combine all three results using the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{kj}} = -(t_k - o_k) \cdot f'(net_k) \cdot h_j$$

**Step 5:** Define the output error signal $\delta_k$:

$$\delta_k \;\equiv\; (t_k - o_k)\, f'(net_k)$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{kj}} = -\delta_k \cdot h_j$$

**Step 6:** Apply gradient descent (move opposite to gradient to *minimize* $E$):

$$\Delta w_{kj} = -\eta \cdot \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{kj}} = -\eta \cdot (-\delta_k h_j) = \eta \, \delta_k \, h_j$$

**Final result:**
$$\boxed{\Delta w_{kj} = \eta \, \delta_k \, h_j}$$

This is the **famous Generalized Delta Rule** of Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986). Every KTU exam question on training algorithms is essentially asking the student to derive this expression.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Hidden-Layer Error Signal $\delta_j$

We now derive why the error at a hidden neuron $j$ is:
$$\delta_j = \left(\sum_{k} w_{kj} \delta_k\right) f'(net_j)$$

The error $E$ depends on hidden neuron $j$ only through its downstream effect on all output neurons $k$. Hence:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial h_j} = \sum_{k=1}^{L} \frac{\partial E}{\partial o_k} \cdot \frac{\partial o_k}{\partial net_k} \cdot \frac{\partial net_k}{\partial h_j}$$

We already know:
- $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial o_k} = -(t_k - o_k)$
- $\dfrac{\partial o_k}{\partial net_k} = f'(net_k)$
- $\dfrac{\partial net_k}{\partial h_j} = w_{kj}$

Therefore:
$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial h_j} = -\sum_{k=1}^{L} (t_k - o_k)\, f'(net_k)\, w_{kj} = -\sum_{k=1}^{L} w_{kj} \delta_k$$

Now apply the chain rule once more to get $\partial E / \partial w_{ji}$:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ji}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial h_j} \cdot \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial net_j} \cdot \frac{\partial net_j}{\partial w_{ji}}$$

Substituting:
$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ji}} = \left(-\sum_{k} w_{kj} \delta_k\right) \cdot f'(net_j) \cdot x_i$$

Define the hidden-layer error signal:
$$\delta_j \;\equiv\; \left(\sum_{k=1}^{L} w_{kj} \delta_k\right) f'(net_j)$$

Then:
$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ji}} = -\delta_j \cdot x_i$$

Gradient descent gives the weight update:
$$\Delta w_{ji} = \eta \, \delta_j \, x_i$$

## 3.3 Numerical Example: One Forward–Backward Pass

Consider a tiny 2-input, 2-hidden, 1-output network.

**Given:**
- Inputs: $x_1 = 0.5, \quad x_2 = 0.8$
- Target: $t = 0.4$
- Learning rate: $\eta = 0.5$
- Initial weights (input→hidden): $w_{11} = 0.3, w_{21} = 0.4, w_{12} = 0.5, w_{22} = 0.2$
- Initial weights (hidden→output): $w_{31} = 0.6, w_{41} = 0.7$
- Biases: $b_1 = 0.1, b_2 = 0.2, b_3 = 0.3$ (output)

**Forward Pass:**

$$net_1 = 0.3(0.5) + 0.4(0.8) + 0.1 = 0.57$$
$$h_1 = f(0.57) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.57}} \approx 0.6389$$

$$net_2 = 0.5(0.5) + 0.2(0.8) + 0.2 = 0.61$$
$$h_2 = f(0.61) \approx 0.6479$$

$$net_3 = 0.6(0.6389) + 0.7(0.6479) + 0.3 = 1.1370$$
$$o = f(1.1370) \approx 0.7571$$

**Error:**
$$E = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.4 - 0.7571)^2 \approx 0.0637$$

**Backward Pass (compute deltas):**

Output delta:
$$\delta_3 = (t - o)\, f'(net_3) = (0.4 - 0.7571) \cdot (0.7571)(1 - 0.7571) = -0.3571 \cdot 0.1838 \approx -0.0656$$

Hidden deltas:
$$\delta_1 = (w_{31}\delta_3)\, f'(net_1) = (0.6)(-0.0656)(0.6389)(1-0.6389) \approx -0.00908$$
$$\delta_2 = (w_{41}\delta_3)\, f'(net_2) = (0.7)(-0.0656)(0.6479)(1-0.6479) \approx -0.01052$$

**Weight updates (using $\eta = 0.5$):**

$$w_{31}^{new} = 0.6 + (0.5)(-0.0656)(0.6389) \approx 0.5790$$
$$w_{41}^{new} = 0.7 + (0.5)(-0.0656)(0.6479) \approx 0.6787$$
$$w_{11}^{new} = 0.3 + (0.5)(-0.00908)(0.5) \approx 0.2977$$
$$w_{21}^{new} = 0.4 + (0.5)(-0.00908)(0.8) \approx 0.3964$$
$$w_{12}^{new} = 0.5 + (0.5)(-0.01052)(0.5) \approx 0.4974$$
$$w_{22}^{new} = 0.2 + (0.5)(-0.01052)(0.8) \approx 0.1958$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the examiner gives numerical values and asks "compute new weights", you MUST show the forward pass, compute the error, compute all deltas, and then the weight updates — in that exact order. Skipping steps leads to mark deduction.

## 3.4 Variants of the Training Algorithm (Analytical Matrix)

| Variant | Update Rule | Pros | Cons |
|---------|-------------|------|------|
| **Batch Gradient Descent** | Uses full dataset before update | Stable convergence | Slow for large data |
| **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** | Update after every sample | Fast, escapes local minima | Noisy |
| **Mini-batch GD** | Update after $b$ samples | Balance of both | Needs batch-size tuning |
| **GD with Momentum** | $\Delta w = \eta \delta h + \alpha \Delta w_{old}$ | Faster convergence | Extra hyperparameter |
| **Adaptive (Adam, RMSProp)** | Per-parameter learning rates | Robust to scaling | More memory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Note:** The **Generalized Delta Rule with Momentum** is the most-frequently-asked variant in KTU 2024 scheme Module 2 questions. The formula is:
> $$w_{kj}(n+1) = w_{kj}(n) + \eta \, \delta_k \, h_j + \alpha \left[w_{kj}(n) - w_{kj}(n-1)\right]$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Backpropagation Training Algorithm — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below captures the **complete functional flow** of the Backpropagation Training Algorithm as applicable to a multi-layer perceptron used in robotic control loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Initialize weights and biases randomly] --> B[Set learning rate eta and momentum alpha]
    B --> C[For each training epoch e from 1 to E_max]
    C --> D[For each input pattern p in training set]
    D --> E[Phase A: FORWARD PASS]
    E --> F[Compute net input at hidden neurons: net_j]
    F --> G[Apply activation function to get hidden output h_j]
    G --> H[Compute net input at output neurons: net_k]
    H --> I[Apply activation to get output o_k]
    I --> J[Compute error E_p using MSE formula]
    J --> K[Phase B: BACKWARD PASS]
    K --> L[Compute output delta: delta_k = t_k - o_k times f prime net_k]
    L --> M[Propagate error to hidden layer: delta_j = sum w_kj delta_k times f prime net_j]
    M --> N[Update hidden-to-output weights: w_kj new = w_kj old plus eta delta_k h_j]
    N --> O[Update input-to-hidden weights: w_ji new = w_ji old plus eta delta_j x_i]
    O --> P[Update biases b_k and b_j using delta signals]
    P --> Q{All patterns processed?}
    Q -- No --> D
    Q -- Yes --> R[Compute total error E_total = sum E_p]
    R --> S{E_total less than threshold OR e equals E_max?}
    S -- No --> C
    S -- Yes --> T[END: Save trained weights for inference]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Backpropagation-Enabled Robotic Vision Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates how the training algorithm integrates into a complete **computer-vision-for-robots** pipeline (the broader context of PBBRT604).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Camera or Sensor Input] --> B[Image Preprocessing: Resize, Normalize, Denoise]
    B --> C[Feature Extractor: Convolutional or MLP Layers]
    C --> D[Forward Pass: Compute Predictions y_hat]
    D --> E[Compare with Ground Truth Labels t]
    E --> F[Compute Loss Function E]
    F --> G[Backward Pass: Backpropagation computes gradients]
    G --> H[Weight Update: Apply Generalized Delta Rule]
    H --> I[Updated Network Weights]
    I --> J{Convergence Achieved?}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Trained Model Deployed on Robot]
    K --> L[Real-time Inference for Navigation or Manipulation]
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Schematic: Error Backpropagation Path

This diagram emphasizes the *direction* of information flow — forward for prediction, backward for error correction.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT
        X1[x1]:::io
        X2[x2]:::io
    end
    subgraph HIDDEN
        H1[h1 = f net 1]:::hidden
        H2[h2 = f net 2]:::hidden
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[o1 = f net 3]:::output
    end
    X1 -->|w11| H1
    X1 -->|w21| H2
    X2 -->|w12| H1
    X2 -->|w22| H2
    H1 -->|w31| O1
    H2 -->|w41| O1
    O1 -.->|delta 3 computed first| ERR[Error Signal Layer]
    ERR -.->|backpropagate delta 1| H1
    ERR -.->|backpropagate delta 2| H2
    classDef io fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef hidden fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show **forward signal flow** (predictions). Dotted arrows show **backward error flow** (gradients). This duality is the defining feature of backpropagation.

## 4.4 Learning-Rate vs. Convergence Trade-off (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Learning Rate eta] --> B{eta value}
    B -- Too small less than 0.01 --> C[Sluggish Convergence: stuck on plateau]
    B -- Moderate 0.1 to 0.5 --> D[Optimal: smooth descent to minimum]
    B -- Too large greater than 1.0 --> E[Oscillation or Divergence]
    C --> F[Increase eta or add momentum]
    D --> G[Continue training]
    E --> H[Reduce eta or normalize inputs]
```
